nodes.name: sender.js
nodes.summary: The Communication Gateway: all outbound messages pass through here with release tiers and autonomy enforcement.

nodes.name: db.js
nodes.summary: Shared Postgres/PGlite connection module; the single pooled DB client every agent and service uses.

nodes.name: client.js
nodes.summary: Gateway to the Postgres task graph: structured work items, typed DAG edges, atomic transitions.

nodes.name: queries.js
nodes.summary: Parameterized SQL query layer over the agent task graph and knowledge graph.

nodes.name: chunker.js
nodes.summary: Splits source documents into embeddable chunks for the RAG pipeline.

nodes.name: retriever.js
nodes.summary: Retrieves relevant chunks from the pgvector RAG knowledge base to ground agent context.

nodes.name: agent-loop.js
nodes.summary: The core agent runtime: polls the task graph, loads context, invokes the LLM, and writes results back as state transitions.

nodes.name: event-bus.js
nodes.summary: Fans task-graph state changes out over pg_notify so downstream agents wake on new work.

nodes.name: guard-check.js
nodes.summary: Enforces constitutional gates G1-G11 atomically with each state transition — the infrastructure-level guardrail boundary (P2).

nodes.name: signal-detector.js
nodes.summary: Extracts actionable signals from the inbox stream for briefings and follow-up tasks.

nodes.name: state-machine.js
nodes.summary: Defines and executes legal work-item state transitions (created->assigned->in_progress->review->completed) with retry/escalation.

nodes.name: embedder.js
nodes.summary: Learns the user's writing voice by embedding past replies so drafts match their tone.

nodes.name: index.js
nodes.summary: Org-level code-generation agent that turns work items into pull requests.

nodes.name: index.js
nodes.summary: Org-level message classification agent — the first stage of the pipeline.

nodes.name: index.js
nodes.summary: Org-level pipeline coordination agent, channel-agnostic.

nodes.name: index.js
nodes.summary: Org-level review agent performing gate checks and quality assurance.

nodes.name: architect.js
nodes.summary: Daily analysis and briefing agent for the inbox product.

nodes.name: orchestrator.js
nodes.summary: Sequences the inbox pipeline: intake -> triage -> responder, coordinating via the task graph.

nodes.name: reviewer.js
nodes.summary: Runs gate/quality checks on drafted replies before egress; one round of feedback then escalate.

nodes.name: api.js
nodes.summary: autobot-inbox HTTP API server (preview.staqs.io) consumed by the board and integrations.

nodes.name: index.js
nodes.summary: Product entry point: applies pending SQL migrations on boot and starts the configured autobot-inbox agents.

nodes.name: layout.tsx
nodes.summary: Root layout for the Next.js 15 Board Workstation; shell and navigation for all governance surfaces.

nodes.name: page.tsx
nodes.summary: Board Workstation landing page (board.staqs.io), the primary human entry point.

nodes.name: page.tsx
nodes.summary: The Today surface: overnight agent digest, drafts awaiting approval, and unresolved signals.

layers.name: Infrastructure (lib/)
layers.description: Org-level shared infrastructure in lib/: the Postgres task graph that coordinates all agents, the runtime agent loop and state machine, guardrail enforcement (constitutional gates G1-G11), the RAG pipeline, channel adapters, communication gateway, and the LLM provider abstraction. Boring, reusable plumbing every product builds on.

layers.name: Org Agents (agents/)
layers.description: Org-level, channel-agnostic agents reusable across products: intake classification, code generation, ticketing, blueprints, redesign, research, plus the reviewer and architect roles. These coordinate through the task graph and are independent of any single product or channel.

layers.name: Product: autobot-inbox
layers.description: autobot-inbox — the first product Optimus operates. Inbox-specific agents (orchestrator, triage, responder, strategist), the Gmail integration, voice-learning, signal extraction/briefings, the HTTP API server, and the SQL migrations that define the inbox schema.

layers.name: Board Workstation (board/)
layers.description: The Board Workstation — a Next.js 15 dashboard (board.staqs.io) where the human board governs the agent organization: Today, Drafts, Signals, Pipeline, Governance, Activity, and the Workstation surfaces. The primary human interface to the system.

tour.title: The Task Graph: Single Source of Truth
tour.description: Everything in Optimus is coordinated through a Postgres task graph, not a message queue. lib/graph/client.js and queries.js are the gateway to structured work items with typed DAG edges and atomic state transitions. No email, no Redis queue between agents — just SQL rows and edges. Hold this in mind: every agent in the tour reads and writes here.
tour.languageLesson: Parameterized SQL only — Optimus never interpolates strings into queries (design principle P1/P2), so the query layer is a thin, audited gateway rather than an ORM.

tour.title: The Database Connection
tour.description: lib/db.js is the one Postgres/PGlite connection module the whole org shares. Events flow over pg_notify rather than an external broker (principle P4: boring infrastructure). Every state transition and audit row lands here, hash-chained and append-only.
tour.languageLesson: A single pooled pg client exported as a module singleton keeps connection counts predictable — critical when 20 agents poll concurrently.

tour.title: The Agent Loop: How an Agent Runs
tour.description: lib/runtime/agent-loop.js is the heartbeat of every agent. It polls the task graph for claimable work, loads context, invokes the agent's LLM, and writes results back as a state transition. This is the runtime that turns a static config row into a living agent.
tour.languageLesson: A poll-loop over claim_next_task() (SELECT ... FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED) lets many agent processes share one queue without a coordinator.

tour.title: The State Machine: Legal Transitions
tour.description: Work items move created -> assigned -> in_progress -> review -> completed. lib/runtime/state-machine.js defines which transitions are legal and executes them. Failed tasks retry up to 3 times, then escalate. The agent loop never mutates state directly — it asks the state machine.
tour.languageLesson: Encoding allowed transitions as data (a transition table) rather than scattered if-statements makes the whole lifecycle auditable in one place.

tour.title: Guardrails: Infrastructure Enforces, Prompts Advise
tour.description: lib/runtime/guard-check.js is where Optimus's constitutional gates G1-G11 live: budget pre-authorization, commitment detection, voice-tone matching, rate limiting, prompt-injection screening. guardCheck() and the state transition run as ONE atomic Postgres transaction — agents cannot self-police past it (principle P2).
tour.languageLesson: Wrapping the guard check and the state write in a single DB transaction makes the security boundary the database, not the prompt — exactly P2.

tour.title: The Event Bus: How Work Propagates
tour.description: When a task changes state, lib/runtime/event-bus.js fans the notification out via pg_notify so the next agent in the pipeline wakes up. This is how an intake classification becomes a triage task becomes a draft — no orchestrator polling a clock.
tour.languageLesson: pg_notify/LISTEN gives near-real-time event delivery with zero extra infrastructure — the same Postgres that stores the graph also routes its events.

tour.title: Retrieval: Grounding Agents in Knowledge
tour.description: Agents do not hallucinate context — they retrieve it. lib/rag/retriever.js pulls relevant chunks from the RAG knowledge base (pgvector embeddings) so an agent answering a board question or drafting a reply is grounded in real org knowledge.
tour.languageLesson: Cosine-similarity search over pgvector keeps retrieval inside Postgres — no separate vector DB to operate.

tour.title: Org Agents: The Reusable Roster
tour.description: agents/ holds channel-agnostic agents reusable across products. agents/executor-intake classifies inbound messages; agents/orchestrator coordinates the pipeline. They are pure roles — they read and write the task graph and know nothing about Gmail or Slack specifically.
tour.languageLesson: Keeping agents channel-agnostic (no Gmail imports) is what lets a second product reuse the same roster — the coupling lives in the product layer, not here.

tour.title: The Product: autobot-inbox API Server
tour.description: autobot-inbox/src/index.js boots the product and autobot-inbox/src/api.js serves the HTTP API (preview.staqs.io) the board and integrations call. On boot it applies pending SQL migrations and starts the configured agents. This is where the org-level infrastructure becomes a running product.
tour.languageLesson: Running migrations on boot (tracked in a _migrations table) means a fresh deploy self-heals its schema before serving a request.

tour.title: Inbox Agents: Orchestrator and Reviewer
tour.description: The product wires concrete agents: autobot-inbox/src/agents/orchestrator.js sequences the intake -> triage -> responder pipeline, and reviewer.js runs gate checks on drafted replies before anything leaves. The reviewer gets one round of feedback, then escalates.
tour.languageLesson: A reviewer agent with read-only access to executor work enforces separation of duties at the agent level, mirroring a human review gate.

tour.title: Voice and Signal: The Inbox's Senses
tour.description: Two product subsystems give autobot-inbox its character: lib/voice/embedder.js learns the user's writing voice so drafts sound like them, and lib/runtime/signal-detector.js surfaces actionable signals from the inbox stream into briefings.
tour.languageLesson: Embedding the user's past replies and matching tone at draft time is cheaper and more faithful than prompt-only "write like me" instructions.

tour.title: The Communication Gateway: Controlled Egress
tour.description: Nothing reaches a human or external party except through lib/comms/sender.js, the Communication Gateway with outbound release tiers. This is the choke point where a drafted reply, once approved, actually sends — and where the autonomy level is enforced.
tour.languageLesson: Routing all egress through one gateway makes "deny by default" (P1) enforceable: if it does not pass the gateway, it never leaves the system.

tour.title: The Board Workstation: Human Governance
tour.description: board/src/app/layout.tsx and page.tsx bootstrap the Next.js 15 Board Workstation (board.staqs.io) — the primary human surface. The board reviews drafts, resolves signals, watches the pipeline, and sets governance, closing the loop on the agent organization.
tour.languageLesson: Next.js App Router file-based routing (app/<segment>/page.tsx) maps each governance surface to a folder — the directory tree IS the navigation.

tour.title: The Daily Loop: Today
tour.description: board/src/app/today/page.tsx is where a board member starts the day: the digest of what agents did overnight, drafts awaiting approval, and unresolved signals. It reads the same task graph the agents write to — the human and the agents share one source of truth.
tour.languageLesson: A read-model page that queries the live task graph keeps the human view consistent with agent state with no separate reporting store.
